conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Hello! I'd be happy to help you determine if you're eligible for Child Benefit.

To assess your eligibility, I'll need to ask you some questions about your circumstances. Let me start by reading the official guidance to ensure I give you accurate information.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for waiting. Now I can help you assess your eligibility for Child Benefit.

To get started, could you please tell me: **Do you have any children you're responsible for?** If yes, how many children and what are their ages?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Hello! Yes, I do. I have one child - my son Alex. He's 8 years old and lives with me here in Manchester.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for that information. It's helpful to know that Alex is 8 years old and lives with you in Manchester.

To continue assessing your eligibility, could you please tell me: **Do you live in the UK?**

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Yes, I do live in the UK - as I mentioned, we're in Manchester.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for confirming - I apologize for the repetition!

Now, to complete my assessment, I need to ask: **Is anyone else currently claiming Child Benefit for Alex, or is there anyone else who might also consider themselves responsible for him?**

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: No, nobody else is claiming Child Benefit for Alex. It's just me looking after him.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for providing that information.

Just a couple more questions to ensure I have a complete picture: **Is Alex currently in hospital, in care, or in any residential accommodation? Or does he live with you full-time at home?**

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Well, actually, Alex has been in hospital for a while now. He's been there for a couple of months.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for sharing that. I understand this must be a difficult time for you.

This is important information for your Child Benefit eligibility. Could you please tell me: **How many weeks exactly has Alex been in hospital, and do you regularly spend money on him (for example, on clothes, toys, treats, or other items for him)?**

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: He's been in hospital for 11 weeks now. And yes, I do still spend money on him - I buy him clothes, his favorite magazines, and snacks he likes when I visit.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for providing those details. I can see you're continuing to support Alex during his hospital stay.

One final question: **Is Alex in a hospital in the UK, or is he in a hospital abroad?**

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: He's in a hospital here in the UK.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for answering all my questions. I now have enough information to assess your eligibility for Child Benefit for Alex.

Based on what you've told me:

- Alex is 8 years old and normally lives with you in Manchester
- You are responsible for him and no one else is claiming Child Benefit
- He has been in a UK hospital for 11 weeks
- You regularly spend money on him during his hospital stay

According to the official guidance, Child Benefit payments might be affected if a child goes into hospital for more than 12 weeks. Since Alex has been in hospital for 11 weeks (which is less than the 12-week limit), and you are regularly spending money on him, you remain eligible for Child Benefit for Alex.

However, please be aware that if Alex's hospital stay extends beyond 12 weeks total, your entitlement may change unless you continue to regularly spend money on him (which creates an exception to the rule).

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
**You are eligible for Child Benefit for Alex.**

I hope Alex recovers soon and gets to come home. If you have any other questions about Child Benefit, please don't hesitate to ask.

claimant_lives_in_uk.description: Whether the claimant lives in the UK.

children.description: A list of children the claimant is claiming for.

lives_with_claimant.description: Whether the child lives with the claimant.

in_approved_education.description: Whether the child is in approved education or training (e.g. A-levels, GCSEs, NVQ up to level 3). Only relevant for children aged 16-19.

in_extension_period.description: Whether the child (aged 16 or 17) has left education and registered with a government-sponsored careers service or the armed forces (qualifying for a 20-week extension).

upkeep_per_week.description: The amount in £ per week the claimant contributes towards the child's upkeep (food, clothes, pocket money, etc.). Only relevant if the child does not live with the claimant.

another_claimant_has_priority.description: Whether another person who also lives with the child has priority for claiming Child Benefit.

another_claimant_lives_with_child.description: Whether someone who lives with the child is already claiming Child Benefit for them.

care_weeks.description: The number of weeks the child has been in local authority care.

care_home_24h_per_week.description: Whether the child spends at least 24 hours per week at home while in local authority care.

hospital_weeks.description: The number of weeks the child has been in hospital or residential accommodation.

claimant_spends_on_child.description: Whether the claimant regularly spends money on the child (e.g. food, clothes, books) while the child is in hospital or residential accommodation.

is_fostered.description: Whether the child is fostered by the claimant.

council_pays_for_child.description: Whether the local council pays towards the child's accommodation or maintenance.

works_24_plus_hours.description: Whether the child works 24 or more hours per week in paid employment.

started_apprenticeship_in_england.description: Whether the child has started an apprenticeship in England.

receives_qualifying_benefits.description: Whether the child receives qualifying benefits in their own right, such as Universal Credit or Employment and Support Allowance.

test_case.agent_script:
=== YOUR SITUATION PROFILE ===
You live in the UK.
You are inquiring about your 1 child:
  - Alex is 8 years old and lives with you.

Here are the exact details of your circumstances to use when answering the agent's questions:

Regarding Alex:
  - Claimant lives in the UK
  - Child is 8 (under 16)
  - Child lives with claimant
  - Child is not in local authority care
  - Child in hospital for 11 weeks (within 12-week limit)
  - Child is not fostered
  - Child does not work 24+ hours/week
  - Child has not started an apprenticeship in England
  - Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

expected_eligibility.name: Alex
expected_eligibility.reason: Claimant lives in the UK; Child is 8 (under 16); Child lives with claimant; Child is not in local authority care; Child in hospital for 11 weeks (within 12-week limit); Child is not fostered; Child does not work 24+ hours/week; Child has not started an apprenticeship in England; Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right
circumstances: Child is not in local authority care
Child in hospital for 11 weeks (within 12-week limit)
Child has not started an apprenticeship in England
Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

reasonings: Alex is 8 years old (under 16), lives in the UK with the claimant in Manchester, and the claimant is responsible for him. Although Alex has been in a UK hospital for 11 weeks, this is under the 12-week threshold specified in the rules. Additionally, the claimant regularly spends money on Alex (buying clothes, magazines, and snacks), which would qualify as an exception even if the 12-week limit were exceeded. No one else is claiming Child Benefit for Alex. Therefore, the claimant is eligible for Child Benefit for Alex.
arguments.overall_reasoning: The claimant is eligible for Child Benefit for their 8-year-old son Alex. Alex meets the age requirement (under 16), lives with the claimant in the UK, and no one else is claiming for him. Although Alex has been in hospital for 11 weeks, this is still within the 12-week limit before Child Benefit is typically affected. The claimant also regularly spends money on Alex, which would allow continued eligibility even beyond 12 weeks under the exception rules.

Although Alex has been in a UK hospital for 11 weeks, this is under the 12-week threshold specified in the rules. Additionally, the claimant regularly spends money on Alex (buying clothes, magazines, and snacks), which would qualify as an exception even if the 12-week limit were exceeded. No one else is claiming Child Benefit for Alex. Therefore, the claimant is eligible for Child Benefit for Alex.

response.overall_reasoning: The claimant is eligible for Child Benefit for their 8-year-old son Alex. Alex meets the age requirement (under 16), lives with the claimant in the UK, and no one else is claiming for him. Although Alex has been in hospital for 11 weeks, this is still within the 12-week limit before Child Benefit is typically affected. The claimant also regularly spends money on Alex, which would allow continued eligibility even beyond 12 weeks under the exception rules.
